bibliographic_data.title: Detecting Figures and Part Labels in Patents: Competition-Based Development of Image Processing Algorithms
bibliographic_data.abstract: We report the findings of a month-long online competition in which participants developed algorithms for augmenting the digital version of patent documents published by the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO). The goal was to detect figures and part labels in U.S. patent drawing pages. The challenge drew 232 teams of two, of which 70 teams (30%) submitted solutions. Collectively, teams submitted 1,797 solutions that were compiled on the competition servers. Participants reported spending an average of 63 hours developing their solutions, resulting in a total of 5,591 hours of development time. A manually labeled dataset of 306 patents was used for training, online system tests, and evaluation. The design and performance of the top-5 systems are presented, along with a system developed after the competition which illustrates that winning teams produced near state-of-the-art results under strict time and computation constraints.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper reports on a month-long online competition to develop algorithms for detecting figures and part labels in patent documents, showcasing near state-of-the-art results under strict time and computation constraints.
core_content.key_methodology: Teams developed image processing algorithms to detect figure locations and labels along with part labels in patent drawings. A manually labeled dataset of 306 patents was used for training, testing, and evaluation.
outcomes: 70 teams submitted solutions out of 232 participating teams.
1,797 solutions were compiled on the competition servers.

The top-5 systems' performance metrics are presented, including f-measure values for figure region detection, figure regions with correctly recognized titles, and part label detection and character recognition.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this paper, a month-long online competition was held to develop algorithms for augmenting the digital version of patent documents published by the USPTO. The goal was to detect figures and part labels in U.S. patent drawing pages. Teams spent an average of 63 hours developing their solutions, resulting in a total of 5,591 hours of development time. A manually labeled dataset of 306 patents was used for training, testing, and evaluation. The top-5 systems' performance metrics are presented, including f-measure values for figure region detection, figure regions with correctly recognized titles, and part label detection and character recognition.
